title: Traction and Skeletal Pin Care
subtitle: The nurse maintains traction safely — keeping weights hanging freely, ropes running in the pulleys, the patient in alignment with counter-traction, and assessing pin sites and neurovascular status.

patient.name: Traction Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse cares for a patient in traction with skeletal pins.

labs.r: Pain, pallor, pulse, paresthesia, paralysis, temp.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient in traction, which aligns and immobilizes a fracture and reduces muscle spasm. Skin traction (such as Buck's) uses a boot or straps with lighter weight and is usually temporary, while skeletal traction uses pins or wires inserted into the bone with heavier weights and must be maintained continuously and never interrupted. The weights are kept hanging freely off the floor and are not lifted or removed; the ropes run freely in the pulleys with secure, unfrayed knots; and the patient is kept in proper alignment with counter-traction so they do not slide down in bed. Pin sites are assessed for infection (redness, drainage, odor, or loosening) and cleaned per protocol. The nurse performs neurovascular checks distal to the injury using the 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulse, paresthesia, paralysis, and temperature) and inspects the skin over pressure points. Because of immobility, the patient is encouraged to exercise unaffected limbs and is monitored for DVT, pneumonia, and constipation.
providerOrders: Keep traction weights hanging freely off the floor
Maintain skeletal traction continuously; do not remove the weights
Keep ropes in the pulleys with secure knots and the patient in alignment
Perform pin-site care per protocol and monitor for infection
Perform neurovascular checks (6 Ps) distal to the injury

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about traction care?

opts.text: Traction aligns a fracture and reduces muscle spasm
The weights must hang freely and not touch the floor
Skeletal traction is continuous and not interrupted
The nurse may remove the weights to reposition the patient
Ropes should move freely in the pulleys with secure knots
Pin sites are assessed for signs of infection
Weights resting on the floor stop the pulling force
Counter-traction keeps the patient from sliding down in bed
Redness, drainage, and odor at a pin site suggest infection
Neurovascular checks detect poor circulation distal to the injury
Lifting the weights briefly maintains correct traction
Frayed ropes or knots caught in the pulley impair traction
Verify the weights hang freely off the floor
Check ropes and pulleys for free movement and secure knots
Assess body alignment and counter-traction
Perform a neurovascular check distal to the injury
Inspect the pin sites and document
Applied with a boot or straps to the skin
Pins or wires inserted into the bone
Uses lighter weight and is often temporary
Requires sterile pin-site care
May be released intermittently if specifically ordered
Must be maintained continuously and never interrupted
Used short-term before surgery for a hip fracture
Allows heavier weights for longer-term pull

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A pin site is red with purulent drainage and the patient reports increased pain
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the pin site, neurovascular status, and vital signs
Check for fever and other signs of infection
Notify the provider of a suspected pin-site infection
Perform prescribed pin care or obtain a culture as ordered
Document the findings
Letting the weights hang freely off the floor
Resting the weights on the bed to reposition
Maintaining continuous skeletal traction
Removing skeletal weights to turn the patient
Performing pin-site care and watching for infection
Ignoring a pale, cool, pulseless foot below the injury
Keeping the patient aligned with counter-traction
Lifting the weights by hand while moving the bed

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
